speaker: interviewer
text: Borys Eisenstein, November 30th, 1995-- you were talking about your surgery in Italy and how eventually you made a decision to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was very sick. They did everything they could in Vienna. I was there for three months in the hospital. And they-- and they told they can do nothing to me. Everything is burned, poisoned. I need new stomach, new [? gastrica. ?] And I didn't know what a [? gastrica ?] is. That's the pipe, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyways, the guys brought me to Italy to the Alps. And they took me to Rome in the Jewish ghetto, hospital. They brought me to the Jewish hospital in Rome in the ghetto. And I didn't like it. And every time they're bringing up a guy or woman from there, next day, they're dead. And I snucked up to see what's going on. Was this little guy, I find out from the nurses right away. I spoke a little bit Italian. And-- and they told me he's come from New York. He was-- What do you call him? In the back. He was-- Hunchback?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was having a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hunchback?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you know-- a [NON-ENGLISH], you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a little guy-- he was-- he was in a chair. And the nurses say he's a butcher. I think he come for training there. And next guy they took got ulcer too. And [? after ?] tomorrow, I have to go. And he got up. He was happy, the guy. And then in middle of night [INAUDIBLE], I want to talk to him. And he's dead. So I grabbed my clothes. I don't know if it was my clothes or your clothes or who knows whose clothes it was. And I took off. I ran away from the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he check the-- I check with the train to Milan, [INAUDIBLE]. You know? And I know the general stuff, what it is. The guys told me. They took me there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them the whole story. So they sent me to secret place from the Haganah. And I start to be on a diet and was OK. I didn't feel so bad. And the guys need me so badly from the Haganah they didn't have to trust guys. I don't know why they put the trust in me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the vendor made my papers for Rome district and for Milan district. And every time, they sent a different mission. I didn't know who sent me. I didn't know for what sent me. What they send me, they send me. And I did perfectly everything. And then I got in a very bad shape via [INAUDIBLE] already. When I was traveling, I didn't have the right food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I did vino, you know? And I'm not supposed to do. I did lots of things and I got very sick [INAUDIBLE]. So I went back to the point. And they took me to the recommended, this professor.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he-- this was a private clinic and he didn't have license. He was a fascist. But ballerinas, models used to turn over their stomachs. They used to bring it to him, and he used to operate on them. He got [INAUDIBLE] me. He sent me right away for x-rays to Milan. He paid for it. I didn't have money. And I went in the name of [INAUDIBLE]. They made me not in my name, made me [INAUDIBLE] papers, [? Italian. ?] And he send me-- he sent me to Milan himself. He paid for it, gave me my ticket, and he gave me the doctor address and they made x-rays. I come back right away. They made x-rays. I come back and he says to me, it's very bad. We have to operate it right away. I'm short on blood. Anyways, so he finds me a donor. I got friends right away, a few Italian friends. And they donate-- they volunteer to give me blood. And they find it was Prince [INAUDIBLE], from the princes [? in ?] the villa. I got pictures with him. His-- his blood was good. So they give me 60 cubic. They put him alive in a table, me on a table. And they put in needles with pipes in him and in me and like that-- 60 cubics. And they took me to the operation table. And nuns-- and they give me the freezing [INAUDIBLE] for me. I was so weak. Like I said, they give me blood. And he operate four and a half hours. And he was very [INAUDIBLE]. You know, what they did to me-- you know, everything was-- and he took out the stomach, and he showed me on a-- on a-- what do you call them, in a silver plate. I'm not-- not telling you, you know, lies or, you know. And after the surgery-- I passed out in the middle of the surgery. And they gave me a needle here in my heart. A nun-- was nuns there. And there was an Israel representative like a, you know, representative from Israel. A doctor, one from Milan-- a woman doctor-- and two guys from the Haganah. [INAUDIBLE] he says, he don't want [? me ?] blame him. He was a fascist. He says, they can put him in jail. He's not going to operate with me. Want to be witnesses. So they give him Jewish two doctors. And after the surgery put me in a dark room. And he says, three days I have to be in a dark room-- dark complete and ventilator. And nuns was 24 hours beside me and etched up, you know high-- not to get pneumonia. You explained me. And he call me [ITALIAN], my son. And after third day, the nuns coming in with the professor with a silver plate. I'll never forget it-- two chops, real chops, cauliflower in the middle, and I think some beans or potato.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he rolled it up. And he was very strict to me. And I says to him, what's going on? I didn't eat it for years. He says to me, [ITALIAN], you know-- eat. And he swear to me. And he feed me. And then I didn't smoke. Then he gave me a cigarette. And he told me [ITALIAN]. And I says why? He says, treat. I ask why. He says, that's good. Tomorrow, if you're going to [INAUDIBLE], this is going to [INAUDIBLE] your stomach. You understand? And he left. And they closed dark again. In the morning, I start to scream. You know what? Nuns were there. But I start to scream. I wanted to have my, you know, stomach. And that's-- he-- he took it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told the nuns-- I heard. Italians speak well and I understand well. And he told me-- he told them he wanted to have the [INAUDIBLE]. He want to make himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ask why. He says, if it's not bleeding, you know? If you don't-- and he says to me, I'm going to send you back to fight for Israel. [LAUGHS] And so how long after that did you go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight months? And in was eight months, you were doing your regular Haganah activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I come to Israel, they-- I told him what happened. After the seizure, I was still with band-aids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's going on in there? They took me with band-aids. And they send me to the [INAUDIBLE] hospital and [INAUDIBLE] hospital, British [INAUDIBLE] hospital. They send me to there. And I was mobilized in the army automatically, you know, but already, you know, the army hospital automatically, you know? Soldier in a hospital. They kept me there for a few months, and they sent me out to the unit. Do you remember the date that you arrived in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I got the papers. I got the stamp. If I show it to you, I don't remember-- I think in August, 28th of August, 1948. Yeah, I got the stamp, you know, when-- when we come to Israel to put a stamp for immigration, you know, when they arrive, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent time-- after you recovered, you joined the Israeli army. And how long were you in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1949. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Special unit in 1949. And that's-- after that, they told me if I want to sign up for the Army, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, you sign up there. They send you to courses, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sign up for eight or 10 years, I don't know. Or you wanted something else, you know? And I didn't know what I want. I've got no family, no place to go. So but I want to give Israel more what I can do. And was in the radio and the papers. They got no police and-- you-- you understand? And they need specialists, chauffeurs, you know? Gun fighters, like, you know-- guys where they know what the weapon is all about it. And so I volunteer again to the force. And the first day when I volunteered, they send me to the border. And which border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jordanian-Syrian border. And that's I call nighttime there. First night, first policeman, I come in. Just, was nobody there-- was one policeman. And ask him what to do. And he says, he does nothing to all-- and the border is lots of trouble. Finally, other come in with a mule. And he says to them, to the policeman, he says we got shootings in a village-- lots of trouble. The village was-- this village particular was half to Jordan, half to Israel [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know Arabic. I don't know English. He-- he told me to take a truck and go to see what's going on there, you understand? That-- that's the first night. The Arab takes the mule, and I go-- go after him with the truck. Don't think I've got a gun. Didn't have a weapon, nothing else-- just the gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come into the village, [? hill ?] site. And I couldn't go with the trucks or [INAUDIBLE] and I walking. They're shooting each other-- one site to the other side, screening Arabic. I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know the language. I walked down to the fighting area and I started scream, I don't know, Russian, Polish, who knows? Japanese-- I have no idea what I scream for. You know? But I didn't touch the gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And stopped fighting. And they start to call me. And they speak to me Arabian. And I don't know nothing. They start to speak English. I says, I don't know. So they put me-- sit down. I sit down. They told me to take off the belt. I put the belt and the weapon there. They start to serve me coffee. They start to bring food. They start to oh oh, you know, the Arabs-- in the [? shade ?] tent in the [INAUDIBLE]. And we're sitting and having coffee here. From the [? hill site, ?] I left my truck, an army unit, tanks, you know-- jeeps. From the-- soldiers, real Israeli army come in to help me. [LAUGHS] And I can't radio, you know, what's going down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent just one policeman. And here, I am sitting having with them coffee. So the Israeli commander comes down. They start to speak to me. He says, you speak Arabic? You speak-- he says no. And he says, [INAUDIBLE] kind of funny. I says, I've watched lots of cowboys movies.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did like a cowboy. [LAUGHS] And he says, but you did it. You know, they would kill you, like, you know, [? what is ?] for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, you see I survived. And I'm having coffee with them. So then I come back there. So they send [? the ?] report. They got the report, what's going on. So right away, they grab me in the headquarters, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They grabbed me to the headquarters. And then was the chief of intelligence [INAUDIBLE]. You know, was a [INAUDIBLE]. He was working for the police intelligence. And he says, you're going to work with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in that position?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that position, he even chose my wife, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. He didn't let me get married just like that. So he went to investigate. I told him, you know, I worked with him a year, a year and a half. And then I told him I want to get married. And he says, I'm not gonna let you just get married. He says, I want to see with who you're getting married. And he went to my wife's family. And he OKed the marriage. He OKed marriage and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in the [INAUDIBLE] surprise. So the-- in Haifa, the Israel restaurant hotel-- name was Israel. He give me the hotel free with all food, services, everything in the house. And they blocked-- they blocked two streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many guests we got-- from the army, from the force. I don't know how many. But to me was [INAUDIBLE] from the Negev and from [INAUDIBLE], from all over-- army unit, police unit. And I think the king didn't have a wedding like that. And your bride's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your bride's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My bride's name was Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edna--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dimmerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dimmerman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my uncle come then from [? Natanya. ?] You know what I told my mother's brother? And he brought a dish for my wife and a punch cake, a gift. This was a big gift. But in my reading was-- from intelligence, from Syrian, Lebanon, and Jordanian officers with clothes [INAUDIBLE] not me, my chief, you know, [? Geffen, ?] my [INAUDIBLE] before the state of Israel. It was a [? sir. ?] And he took his name. It changed his name from [? Geffen. ?] It's the same-- same interpretation, yeah. He was a [? sir. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he organized them to come to my wedding in civil clothes. We still got the gifts from them-- coffee mugs and-- what is that? Yeah, and my mother's picture, yeah, and coffee mugs, silverware. But we don't have the wedding pictures. And I think-- I'm sorry. I think of that, the wedding picture was making-- they didn't want it-- mine was organized [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't want it to get in the pictures. But that table were so high, the [INAUDIBLE] people in Jordan, you know, Syrian, Lebanon, from intelligence [? by ?] the table sitting with Israeli officers, intelligent, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't want to embarrass them. So he took a photograph from our whole unit, a girl. She made the pictures. Somehow, I don't know, she's dead and no pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: In time for our wedding. Three days after that, they find you dead-- burnt, you know, in your apartment and no pictures. I don't know if yes was pictures. I don't know-- no pictures. But we didn't get the pictures. And the girl, poor girl-- they kill you. And that was my present. And we were-- we got married. We went to the centers to [INAUDIBLE] beautiful home-- in Hadera, excuse me. In Hadera, they remodeled a beautiful apartment for us in the police station, just eight apartments, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the chief police, we got-- only-- I got off in a few days for honeymoon like. We couldn't have honeymoon. We didn't have money or nothing. We didn't have a place to go. So the chief of police have us his limousine, give it to me a gift for two weeks to travel Israel. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Give me three-- two weeks off, his limousine, to go with my wife. So we went to Tel Aviv. We went places. You know, family we find, friends. We spend the two weeks with this limousine. We come back and our apartment was beautiful apartment. And my wife got pregnant. She got a job, kindergarten. We were making-- there was 28 pounds a month. Was for one week to buy food-- her wages, my wages. So we couldn't survive. I went to work in [? parks, ?] for a taxi driver, you name it-- with permission. And my wife got a beautiful job. But she got pregnant, and she couldn't carry on. It was very heavy pregnancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they transferred me to [? Karkur. ?] This was the border, Jordan border. We have to sleep on the floor on account-- we got beautiful furniture-- on account the Arabs every night invading the station. But, you know, the terrorists.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have water. We didn't have hydro. We survive. We liked it. And my daughter-- our daughter was born. We didn't have water to bathe here. So the kibbutzim, I went into the kibbutzim, and they gave me jugs of milk. And we bathe our daughter and ourselves in milk. You know. And we didn't have hydro, just a [INAUDIBLE]. Until our daughter got two years old, and she was [INAUDIBLE]. She want to speak French, English. She got nobody to play with, nobody [? to that. ?] So I ask for a transfer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they transfer me to Haifa. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they-- I worked in Haifa in, what do call them, special units and [? cruisers-- ?] special units. And we lived in [INAUDIBLE]. Beautiful apartment too they give us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the only thing trouble was the utilities was with another police family. This was not comfortable, but we survived till I got healed. And I says I have to, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Not enough ?] dead, they want to send me to the Negev, while they start to develop Negev police stations, you know, to build up new development. And my wife's family was against this. I could give you a beautiful job, she could, in the city, you know? Kindergarten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I got healed. And the doctor told me-- I start to suffer there in the knee and here in the back. And I went every day for treatment. And that was no good for me. I figure up maybe they're going to fire me. You know, [INAUDIBLE] lots of people want my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided, you know, I got a family here-- to try appeal for a few months. A doctor told me, OK, change your [INAUDIBLE] see what happened. That's what happened. So I took three months leave at half pay. Then I start to be good. I took another two years' leave. And that's-- that's when you came to Winnipeg in 1956?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then I prolonged my leave. And a while, after 10 years, you got the right for two years' leave without pay. My wife was living in the station. She didn't have to pay the rent, you know, with the daughter. And I sent how much I could, you know. Plus, you've got your own family. They support you.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1958, they didn't let up my wife. They want me to go back. The consulate come a few times to persuade me to go back. And I told them that's not a job for something that thinks is my life, my health. So then they let my wife out, you know? They brought my wife. And that was a 1958?

speaker: interviewee
text: '58, Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing when she arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in a cutter, Jacob and [? Crowley, ?] S and S Sportwear, you name it. Was season work. Hardly could survive-- $18 a week and unemployment insurance, you know? Three months, you work, and three months, you collect it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you buy your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife start to push me. She said, there's no end to it. She was working very hard, two jobs. And my wife told me was going to be the end. And here [INAUDIBLE] city for my wife's city. In my city, they promise if I go in business, they shake hands with me, they're going give me money-- $1,000 or $500 to help me out. Anyways, they didn't give it to me-- nothing. So when I bought the business, I got $1,000 we got from Germany. I got it paid from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So put a $1,000 down payment to Gordon [? Pollock, ?] friend of mine then, not now. [LAUGHS] He was then a friend of mine, a good friend. And he was then having his business in [? basement ?] [INAUDIBLE] main, not to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways-- and I was [? sure to get-- ?] I need $3,000 down payment to buy the business. You know, and after a few weeks, I went to the [? landsman. ?] They didn't give it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They laughed at me. They threw me out from the business. So I went to Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. I worked for him. And I told him the whole story. And he says, why are you going to these banks? You know, they're not human.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go and get a business. Tell them I pay for it. And he-- I went. I told Gordon [? Pollack ?] in the mortgage company Mr. [INAUDIBLE] pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bought a car and we bought the business. Gordon [? Pollack ?] didn't took a penny. And Gordon [? Pollack ?] [INAUDIBLE] bought us the house, this house which we live now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it brought me to the business-- five keys to choose, five homes, what kind we wanted. We didn't have money. He says, don't worry about money. Just here, choose one. You'll like it. And sleep there [INAUDIBLE], then get out. There's the way we [? wanted. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we bought a little, little one-- store. We lived there. And the store was a beautiful suite. We rented out the store for $175 and our mortgage here was $175.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we broke even. And thank to God my wife-- she helped me to pay up the bills, the mortgages. She was making a killing-- $21, $22.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last part-time job-- she helped me to survive through the business. And after two-- after four years-- like I said, we-- after four years, we built a big huge supermarket. I got contract with the army and with Indian Affairs. And they give me big orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you retired when?

speaker: interviewee
text: We retired [INAUDIBLE]. We retired when-- five years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: '89, I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they moved in and they bought us up. I never believed that Indian Affairs-- they buy the stock. They buy the home, everything. I didn't even have a lawyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clean deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clean deal-- they paid for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and we'll finish on the next tape then.
